title: Cleaning and Sterilization MCQ – Chapter 34
source: Understanding Anesthesia Equipment (Dorsch & Dorsch) – Section V: Equipment Care

questions.question: What is an antimicrobial?

options.A: A chemical that only kills viruses
options.B: A chemical or material capable of destroying or inhibiting the growth of microorganisms
options.C: A substance that promotes microbial growth

questions.explanation: An antimicrobial is a chemical or material capable of destroying or inhibiting the growth of microorganisms.

questions.question: What distinguishes an antiseptic from a disinfectant?

options.A: Antiseptics are used on inanimate objects
options.B: Antiseptics can be safely applied to living tissue and are regulated as drugs
options.C: Disinfectants are used on living tissue

questions.explanation: An antiseptic is a chemical germicide that has antimicrobial activity and that can be safely applied to living tissue. Antiseptics are regulated as drugs.

questions.question: What does asepsis refer to?

options.B: A scheme or process that prevents contact with microorganisms

questions.explanation: Asepsis is a scheme or process that prevents contact with microorganisms.

questions.question: What is the difference between a bactericide and a bacteriostat?

options.B: A bactericide kills bacteria while a bacteriostat only prevents bacterial growth without necessarily killing them
options.C: A bacteriostat kills bacteria while a bactericide prevents growth

questions.explanation: A bactericide is a chemical agent or compound that kills bacteria. A bacteriostat is an agent that will prevent bacterial growth but does not necessarily kill the bacteria. Bacteriostatic action is reversible.

questions.question: What is bioburden (bioload, microbial load)?

options.B: The number and types of viable organisms contaminating an object

questions.explanation: Bioburden (Bioload, Microbial Load) is the number and types of viable organisms contaminating an object. The level of bioburden is related to the anatomic site where the device was used.

questions.question: What is a biological indicator used for?

options.B: Monitoring the sterilization process using a standardized, viable population of microorganisms

questions.explanation: A biological indicator is a sterilization-process monitoring device consisting of a standardized, viable population of microorganisms (usually bacterial spores) that are known to be resistant to the mode of sterilization being monitored.

questions.question: What is the ceiling value in occupational health?

options.B: Concentration of an airborne contaminant above which a person should not be exposed even momentarily
options.C: The height of the operating room
options.D: The maximum number of patients per day

questions.explanation: Ceiling Value is the concentration of an airborne contaminant above which a person should not be exposed even momentarily.

questions.question: What is a chemical indicator in sterilization?

options.B: A sterilization-process monitoring device designed to respond with a characteristic and visible chemical or physical change to one or more parameters of a sterilization process

questions.explanation: A chemical indicator is a sterilization-process monitoring device designed to respond with a characteristic and visible chemical or physical change to one or more parameters of a sterilization process.

questions.question: What is a chemical integrator?

options.B: A chemical indicator that reacts to a variety of sterilization parameters

questions.explanation: A chemical integrator is a chemical indicator that reacts to a variety of sterilization parameters.

questions.question: What is a chemosterilizer (chemical sterilant)?

options.A: A device that uses heat for sterilization
options.B: A chemical used for the purpose of destroying all forms of microbiological life, including bacterial spores

questions.explanation: A chemosterilizer (chemical sterilant) is a chemical used for the purpose of destroying all forms of microbiological life, including bacterial spores. The same chemical used for shorter exposure periods and/or at a lower concentration may be used for disinfection.

questions.question: What is the definition of cleaning?

options.B: Removal of foreign material from an item

questions.explanation: Cleaning is the removal of foreign material from an item.

questions.question: What does contamination mean?

options.B: State of actually or potentially having been in contact with microorganisms

questions.explanation: Contamination is the state of actually or potentially having been in contact with microorganisms.

questions.question: What is a critical item?

options.A: An item that only touches intact skin
options.B: An item that penetrates the skin or is in contact with normally sterile areas of the body
options.C: An item used for cleaning only

questions.explanation: A critical item is an item that penetrates the skin or is in contact with normally sterile membranes or areas of the body.

questions.question: What is decontamination?

options.A: A process that renders contaminated inanimate items safe for handling by personnel who are not wearing protective attire
options.B: The same as sterilization

questions.explanation: Decontamination is a process that renders contaminated inanimate items safe for handling by personnel who are not wearing protective attire (i.e., reasonably free of the probability of transmitting infection). Decontamination can range from simple cleaning to sterilization.

questions.question: What is a disinfectant?

options.A: A chemical applied to living tissue
options.B: A chemical germicide formulated to be used on inanimate objects

questions.explanation: A disinfectant is a chemical germicide formulated to be used on inanimate objects.

questions.question: What does high-level disinfection kill?

options.B: All organisms with the exception of bacterial spores and certain species such as Creutzfeldt-Jakob prion

questions.explanation: High-level disinfection is a procedure that kills all organisms with the exception of bacterial spores and certain species, such as the Creutzfeldt-Jakob prion. Most high-level disinfectants can produce sterilization with sufficient contact time.

questions.question: What does intermediate-level disinfection kill?

options.B: Vegetative bacteria, including Mycobacterium tuberculosis, most fungi, and viruses but not bacterial spores

questions.explanation: Intermediate-level disinfection is a procedure that kills vegetative bacteria, including Mycobacterium tuberculosis, most fungi, and viruses but not bacterial spores.

questions.question: What does low-level disinfection kill?

options.B: Most vegetative bacteria (but not M. tuberculosis), some fungi, and viruses but no spores

questions.explanation: Low-level disinfection is a procedure that kills most vegetative bacteria (but not M. tuberculosis), some fungi, and viruses but no spores.

questions.question: What is a disposable item?

options.A: An item that can be reused multiple times
options.B: An item intended for use on one patient during a single procedure

questions.explanation: Disposable items are intended for use on one patient during a single procedure.

questions.question: What is a fungicide?

options.A: An agent that promotes fungal growth
options.B: An agent or process that kills fungi

questions.explanation: A fungicide is an agent or process that kills fungi.

questions.question: What does a mechanical monitor measure during sterilization?

options.B: Time, temperature, humidity, or pressure during a sterilization cycle

questions.explanation: A mechanical monitor (physical monitor, physical indicator) is a sterilizer component that gauges and records time, temperature, humidity, or pressure during a sterilization cycle.

questions.question: What is a noncritical item?

options.A: An item that penetrates the skin
options.B: An item that does not ordinarily touch the patient or only touches intact skin but not mucous membranes
options.C: An item that contacts sterile body areas
options.D: An item that must be sterilized

questions.explanation: A noncritical item is an item that does not ordinarily touch the patient or only touches intact skin but not mucous membranes.

questions.question: What is a nosocomial infection?

options.B: An infection acquired in a health care facility
options.D: An infection that cannot be treated

questions.explanation: Nosocomial pertains to a health care facility. A nosocomial infection is one acquired in a health care facility.

questions.question: What is the Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)?

options.A: The maximum time a patient can be in surgery
options.B: The time-weighted average maximum concentration of an air contaminant to which a worker can be exposed according to OSHA standards
options.C: The maximum temperature in a sterilizer

questions.explanation: Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) is the time-weighted average maximum concentration of an air contaminant to which a worker can be exposed according to Occupational Safety and Health Administration (OSHA) standards.

questions.question: What are prions?

options.B: Proteinaceous infectious agents with no associated nucleic acids

questions.explanation: Prions are proteinaceous infectious agents with no associated nucleic acids.

questions.question: What does processing mean in the context of medical devices?

options.B: All of the steps performed to make a contaminated device ready for patient use

questions.explanation: Processing refers to all of the steps performed to make a contaminated device ready for patient use.

questions.question: What is pseudoinfection?

options.B: A positive culture without clinical infection

questions.explanation: Pseudoinfection is a positive culture without clinical infection.

questions.question: What are pyrogens?

options.B: Substances that can produce fever

questions.explanation: Pyrogens are substances that can produce fever.

questions.question: What is reprocessing?

options.B: Decontamination and repackaging of a device that has been used for its intended purpose and is labeled for single use

questions.explanation: Reprocessing is decontamination and repackaging of a device that has been used for its intended purpose and is labeled for single use.

questions.question: What is resterilization?

options.A: First-time sterilization of a new device
options.B: Sterilization of an 'unopened' (i.e., inner wrap still intact) sterile device or an unused wrapped sterile device that is past the expiration date

questions.explanation: Resterilization is sterilization of an 'unopened' (i.e., inner wrap still intact, and therefore, the device is presumably 'unused') sterile device or an unused wrapped sterile device that is past the expiration date.

questions.question: What is a reusable item?

options.A: An item used once and discarded
options.B: An item intended for repeated use either on the same or different patients, with appropriate reprocessing between uses
options.C: An item that cannot be sterilized

questions.explanation: A reusable item is intended for repeated use either on the same or different patients, with appropriate reprocessing between uses.

questions.question: What is sanitization?

options.B: Process of reducing the number of microbial contaminants to a relatively safe level

questions.explanation: Sanitization is the process of reducing the number of microbial contaminants to a relatively safe level. In general, sanitation is used for noncritical surfaces or for applications in which stronger microbial agents may cause device materials to deteriorate.

questions.question: What is a semicritical item?

options.A: An item that penetrates the skin
options.B: A device intended to come in contact with mucous membranes or intact skin but will not normally come into contact with sterile tissues
options.C: An item that only touches intact skin

questions.explanation: A semicritical item is a device that is intended to come in contact with mucous membranes or intact skin but will not normally come into contact with sterile tissues.

questions.question: What is the Short-term Exposure Limit (STEL)?

options.B: A 15-minute time-weighted average exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during a work day
options.C: The minimum contact time for disinfection
options.D: The duration of a sterilization cycle

questions.explanation: Short-term Exposure Limit (STEL) is a 15-minute time-weighted average exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during a work day.

questions.question: What is a spore?

options.B: The normal resting stage in the life cycle of certain bacteria

questions.explanation: A spore is the normal resting stage in the life cycle of certain bacteria.

questions.question: What is a sporicide?

options.A: An agent that promotes spore formation
options.B: An agent that kills spores

questions.explanation: A sporicide is an agent that kills spores.

questions.question: What are Standard Precautions?

options.B: Policies promulgated by the CDC in 1996 that include universal precautions, airborne precautions, droplet precautions, and contact precautions applying to all patients

questions.explanation: Standard Precautions are policies promulgated by the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) in 1996 that include universal precautions, airborne precautions, droplet precautions, and contact precautions. Standard precautions apply to all patients, regardless of their diagnosis or known or presumed infection status.

questions.question: What is a sterilant/disinfectant?

options.B: A term applied by the EPA to a germicide that is capable of sterilization or high-level disinfection

questions.explanation: Sterilant/Disinfectant is a term applied by the Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) to a germicide that is capable of sterilization or high-level disinfection.

questions.question: What is the Sterility Assurance Level (SAL)?

options.B: The probability that microorganisms will survive after a terminal sterilization process
options.C: The number of sterilization cycles needed

questions.explanation: Sterility Assurance Level (SAL) is the probability that microorganisms will survive after a terminal sterilization process. Before a manufacturer can label a product as sterile, it must have a sterility assurance level of 10^-6.

questions.question: What SAL must a product have to be labeled as sterile?

options.B: 10^-6 (no greater than 1 in 1,000,000)

questions.explanation: Before a manufacturer can label a product as sterile, it must have a sterility assurance level of 10^-6, which means that the possibility that microorganisms have survived on the item exists but is no greater than 1 × 10^-6 or 1 in 1,000,000.

questions.question: What is terminal sterilization?

options.B: Sterilization process that is carried out after an item has been placed in its final packaging

questions.explanation: Terminal sterilization is a sterilization process that is carried out after an item has been placed in its final packaging.

questions.question: What is the Threshold Limit Value Ceiling?

options.B: Concentration of an air contaminant to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse effect
options.C: The height limit for storage shelves

questions.explanation: Threshold Limit Value Ceiling is the concentration of an air contaminant to which it is believed that nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed day after day without adverse effect.

questions.question: What is Time-Weighted Average (TWA)?

options.A: The average time for a sterilization cycle
options.B: Integration of all the concentrations of a chemical to which a worker has been exposed during a sampling period, reported as an average

questions.explanation: Time-Weighted Average (TWA) is the integration of all the concentrations of a chemical to which a worker has been exposed during a sampling period, reported as an average.

questions.question: What is a tuberculocide?

options.B: An agent or process that kills tubercle bacilli

questions.explanation: A tuberculocide is an agent or process that kills tubercle bacilli.

questions.question: What agency registers and regulates chemical germicides?

options.B: EPA (Environmental Protection Agency)

questions.explanation: The EPA registers and regulates chemical germicides. It requires manufacturers to test formulations of chemical germicides for microbicidal potency (efficacy), stability, and toxicity. It also approves sterilization devices.

questions.question: What role does the FDA play regarding reusable medical devices?

options.B: It regulates medical devices and requires manufacturers of reusable devices to provide adequate instructions for cleaning and disinfecting or sterilizing

questions.explanation: The FDA regulates medical devices and considers liquid chemical germicides used to reprocess medical instruments as accessories to medical devices. The FDA requires that the manufacturer of a device marketed as reusable provide the user with adequate instructions for cleaning and disinfecting or sterilizing the item.

questions.question: Which organization does NOT approve, regulate, or test germicides or sterilizers but recommends broad strategies to prevent transmission of infections?

options.B: FDA
options.C: CDC (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention)

questions.explanation: The CDC does not approve, regulate, or test germicides or sterilizers. Rather, it recommends broad strategies to prevent transmission of infections in the health care environment.

questions.question: What is the first and most important step in decontamination?

options.B: Thorough cleaning

questions.explanation: The first and most important step in decontamination is thorough cleaning. In some cases, cleaning may be sufficient to render an item suitable for reuse.

questions.question: Why should stainless steel or other metal devices NOT be soaked in saline or sodium hypochlorite solutions?

options.B: The chloride ions in these substances will cause the metal to corrode

questions.explanation: Stainless steel or other metal devices should not be soaked in saline or sodium hypochlorite solutions, because the chloride ions in these two substances will cause the metal to corrode.

questions.question: What should be used to move contaminated devices to the decontamination area?

options.B: A closed container

questions.explanation: A closed container should be used to move contaminated devices to the decontamination area.
